english: S.g.; Sch.: night-hawk, goat-sucker, caprimulgus; bat.
crest, tuft (of feathers) of birds Sch.

Cs.: 'heed, care, caution'. This word belonging to the language of the people and to later literature, is not so much an abstract, as a concrete noun, signifying a watchman, superintendent (chiefly by day, cf. mél-tse (mel tse) night-watch); it denotes more particularly that individual of a community, who has to see to it, that the compulsory post-office duties be punctually performed, and that messages from the lord or magistrate of the place be duly dispatched and forwarded to their place of destination; in a more gen. sense byá-ra byéd-pa (bya ra byed pa) Glr., *čó-če* (*co ce*) W., gtoṅ-ba (gtong ba) Mil. c. la, to give heed, to pay attention, to look sharp, not to lose sight of; also, to be on one's guard against, to take a thing seriously, e.g. nád-la (nad la) a disease Lt.; *já-ra ì-míg* (*ja ra i mig*) (prop. yid-mig (yid mig)) *čo* (*co*), pay strict attention! W.
crow, raven, mentioned in S.O. as an inveterate enemy of the _ug-pa ('ug pa) (owl).
labour, work, zin-pa-méd-pai bya-lás (zin pa med pa'i bya las) endless labours Mil.
1. Cs. pliancy, nimbleness, agility of body; byag-mkán (byag mkhan) rope-dancer Lex. — 2. sometimes erron. for jag (jag) and jàg-pa (jag pa).

1. north; byaṅ-pyógs (byang phyogs) and prob. also byaṅ-k'a (byang kha) Mil. id.; byaṅ-gi (byang gi), byaṅ-pyógs-kyi (byang phyogs kyi) northern; byaṅ-ṅós (byang ngos) north side, northern brow or slope of a hill; also n. pr., Glr.; byaṅ-táṅ (byang thang) n. pr. the heaths or steppes in northern Tibet, more esp. those bordering in the west on Ld. — 2. northern country, coinciding with byaṅ-táṅ (byang thang): byaṅ-la (byang la) bčúg-go (bcug go) he was banished to the north country Glr.; byaṅ-pa (byang pa) a man from Jaṅ-táṅ (jang thang) — 3. the significations of byaṅ-snyom-pa (byang snyom pa) Sch. to tailor, to cut to a proper shape, and of several other compounds, require a different etymology yet unknown. — 4. for byaṅ-bu (byang bu).

1. the inside of the body, byaṅ-kóg-stód (byang khog stod) the upper part of the body, cavity of the chest, byaṅ-kóg-smád (byang khog smad) lower part of the belly, abdomen, bowels S.g.; *jaṅ-kóg-la zug rag* (*jang khog la zug rag*) I feel a pain in my bowels W. — 2. rump; opp. to yan-lág (yan lag) limbs Lt.
the northern continent of the ancient geography of India, v. gliṅ (gling).

बोधि, prop. wisdom; with the Buddhists the highest perfection and holiness, such as every Buddhist desires to obtain, which however to its full extent only the real Buddha himself possesses, v. Köpp. I, 425, 435; byaṅ-čub-mčóg (byang chub mchog) id., frq.; byaṅ-čub-mčóg-tu sems (byang chub mchog tu sems) (or resp. ťugs (thugs)) skyéd-pa (skyed pa) to create the thought of such holiness, to direct the mind to it Dzl., Glr.; byaṅ-čub ºdód-pa (byang chub 'dod pa) to aim at it, to be anxious to obtain it Dzl.; lén-pa (len pa) to attain it; byaṅ-čub-séms (byang chub sems) the mind intent on and suited for it, universal charity; snyiṅ-rje-byaṅ-čub-séms-kyis kun blaṅ-nas (snying rje byang chub sems kyis kun blang nas) submitting to every thing with a loving and charitable mind; byaṅ-čub-séms-dpa, (byang chub sems dpa',) बोधिसत्त्व, frq. with the addition of sems-dpa-čén-po (sems dpa' chen po) the saint that has attained the highest station next to Buddha, merely for the welfare of men still tarrying in this world, designated Buddha, as it were; Köp. I, 422; byaṅ-čub-séms-ma (byang chub sems ma) fem. of it Thgr.; byaṅ-čub-śiṅ (byang chub shing), पिप्पल, the bodhi-tree, holy fig-tree, ficus religiosa (not indica), emblem of mercy; byaṅ-čub-snyiṅ-po (byang chub snying po) बोधिमण्ड, n. pr. = rdo-rje-ydan (rdo rje gdan).
